skills.skill: Counting review - up to 10
skills.prompt: This topic covers counting and recognizing numbers up to 10 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). Students should understand one-to-one correspondence, where each object is counted exactly once. Key concepts include: recognizing number symbols (numerals 0-10), understanding that the last number counted tells 'how many', counting objects in various arrangements (lines, arrays, scattered groups), and comparing small groups to determine which has more or fewer. Students should be able to instantly recognize (subitize) small quantities (1-5) without counting. Use concrete objects, pictures, and number lines as visual supports. Vocabulary: count, number, numeral, more, fewer, same, equal, how many. Common misconceptions include skipping objects when counting, counting an object twice, or not understanding that arrangement doesn't change quantity. Activities should involve hands-on counting with manipulatives, matching numerals to quantities, and real-world counting situations appropriate for 6-7-year-olds.

skills.skill: Count to fill a ten frame
skills.prompt: This topic covers using ten frames to count for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). A ten frame is a 2x5 grid that helps students organize counting and develop number sense. Key concepts include filling frames systematically (left to right, top to bottom), recognizing how many dots are shown without counting each one (subitizing), understanding how numbers relate to 5 and 10, and seeing that a full frame equals 10. Vocabulary: ten frame, full, empty, count, how many, more, less, subitize. Common misconceptions include filling frames randomly, miscounting partially filled frames, and not seeing the relationship to 5. Activities should use physical ten frames with counters, matching frames to numerals, and building number sense through repeated ten frame work appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Counting tens and ones - up to 20
skills.prompt: This topic covers Counting tens and ones - up to 20 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students learn to decompose numbers into groups of tens and ones, which is foundational for understanding place value. Key concepts include grouping objects into sets of ten, recognizing that a 'ten' is a bundle of ten ones, and understanding that two-digit numbers are composed of tens and ones. Visual models such as base-ten blocks, ten frames, and place value charts are essential tools. Vocabulary: tens, ones, place value, group, bundle, digit, compose, decompose. Common misconceptions include confusing the tens and ones digits or not understanding that 10 ones equals 1 ten. Activities should include building numbers with manipulatives and writing corresponding equations appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Write numbers as tens and ones up to 20
skills.prompt: This topic covers writing numbers as tens and ones for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students practice expressing numbers in expanded place value form, e.g., 34 = 3 tens and 4 ones. Key concepts include decomposing numbers into place value components, connecting physical models (base-ten blocks) to written representations, and understanding that the same number can be decomposed in multiple ways when regrouping. Vocabulary: tens, ones, write, decompose, expanded form, digit, place value. Common misconceptions include reversing tens and ones digits and not understanding flexible regrouping. Activities should include writing number sentences from block models and converting between forms appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Counting tens and ones - up to 99
skills.prompt: This topic covers Counting tens and ones - up to 99 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). skills.skill: Convert between tens and ones - multiples of ten
skills.prompt: This topic covers Convert between tens and ones - multiples of ten for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students learn to flexibly convert between different place value representations. Key concepts include understanding that 1 ten equals 10 ones, numbers can be represented in multiple equivalent ways using different groupings, and this flexibility is essential for regrouping in addition and subtraction. Vocabulary: convert, regroup, exchange, tens, ones, hundreds, equivalent, trade. Common misconceptions include thinking there is only one way to represent a number and difficulty understanding that regrouping does not change the total value. Activities should involve trading games with base-ten blocks and solving puzzles requiring flexible place value thinking appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Regroup tens and ones - ways to make a number
skills.prompt: This topic covers Regroup tens and ones - ways to make a number for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Regrouping involves exchanging equivalent amounts between place values. Students learn that numbers can be composed and decomposed in multiple ways while maintaining the same total value. For example, 35 can be 3 tens and 5 ones, or 2 tens and 15 ones. Key concepts include understanding that regrouping preserves total value and is foundational for addition with carrying and subtraction with borrowing. Vocabulary: regroup, trade, exchange, compose, decompose, equivalent, bundle. Common misconceptions include believing each number has only one representation and not understanding that regrouping maintains value. Activities should include manipulative-based trading and 'ways to make a number' exercises appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Write numbers as hundreds, tens, and ones - up to 120
skills.prompt: This topic covers Write numbers as hundreds, tens, and ones - up to 120 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students extend place value understanding to include hundreds, writing numbers in expanded form showing the value of each digit. For example, 115 = 100 + 10 + 5. Key concepts include identifying the value of each digit based on its position, converting between standard form and expanded form, and understanding that each place value position is ten times the one to its right. Vocabulary: hundreds, tens, ones, expanded form, standard form, place value, digit, value. Common misconceptions include confusing face value with place value and difficulty with numbers containing zero. Activities should include base-ten block building, writing equations, and number form conversion exercises appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Counting review - up to 20
skills.prompt: This topic covers counting and reviewing numbers up to 20 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). Students extend counting fluency beyond 10, developing familiarity with teen numbers. Key concepts include understanding teen numbers as 'ten and some more,' maintaining one-to-one correspondence with larger sets, counting objects in various arrangements, and connecting counting to numeral recognition for 11-20. Vocabulary: count, teen numbers, eleven through twenty, how many, more, fewer. Common misconceptions include difficulty with teen number names (confusing thirteen/thirty), losing track when counting beyond 10, and not seeing teen numbers as composed of ten and ones. Activities should use double ten frames, counting collections, and numeral matching appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Count on ten frames - up to 40
skills.prompt: This topic covers counting on ten frames up to 40 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). Students use multiple ten frames to represent and count larger quantities, building understanding of grouping by tens. Key concepts include filling complete ten frames and counting remaining ones, skip counting by tens and then adding ones, understanding that each full frame represents 10, and building toward place value understanding. Vocabulary: ten frame, count, full, tens, ones, how many, skip count. Common misconceptions include miscounting across multiple frames and not recognizing that full frames can be skip-counted by tens. Activities should include physical multi-frame counting, matching frame displays to numbers, and building fluency with quantities to 40 appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Counting objects to 100
skills.prompt: This topic covers counting objects to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(C)). Students practice counting large collections of objects accurately. Key concepts include organizing objects into groups for efficient counting, using groups of ten, maintaining accurate count with larger sets, and recording the total. Vocabulary: count, objects, group, organize, total, how many, tens, ones. Common misconceptions include losing track with larger collections, double-counting or skipping objects, and not using grouping strategies. Activities should include counting collections of real objects (buttons, beans, cubes), organizing into groups of ten before counting, and recording totals appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, build from concrete manipulatives to pictorial representations to abstract notation, ensuring 6-7-year-olds develop both conceptual understanding and procedural fluency.

skills.skill: Number lines - up to 100
skills.prompt: This topic covers Number lines - up to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(F)). Number lines are visual tools that represent numbers as points on a line, helping students understand number order, spacing, and relationships. Key concepts include locating and labeling numbers, understanding that numbers increase from left to right, identifying numbers between given points, and using equal intervals. Vocabulary: number line, point, interval, between, before, after, greater than, less than, order. Common misconceptions include not attending to scale, placing numbers without considering equal spacing, and confusion when the line doesn't start at zero. Activities should involve placing numbers on blank number lines, identifying mystery numbers, and using number lines for comparison and ordering tasks appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Counting objects to 120
skills.prompt: This topic covers counting objects to 120 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(C)). Students extend counting skills to quantities beyond 100, understanding that the number sequence continues past the hundred mark. Key concepts include counting past 100 (101, 102... 120), organizing large collections into groups of ten for efficient counting, and understanding that 120 = 12 tens = 1 hundred and 2 tens. Vocabulary: count, hundred, objects, group, organize, beyond one hundred. Common misconceptions include difficulty transitioning past 100 and not knowing number names beyond 100. Activities should include counting large collections, hundred chart extensions, and real-world counting of large quantities appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Ten more or less
skills.prompt: This topic covers Ten more or less for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.5(C)). Students learn to mentally add or subtract 10 from any given number. Key concepts include understanding that adding 10 increases the tens digit by 1 while the ones digit stays the same, and subtracting 10 decreases the tens digit by 1. On a hundred chart, 10 more is directly below and 10 less is directly above. Vocabulary: ten more, ten less, tens digit, ones digit, pattern, mental math, hundred chart. Common misconceptions include changing the ones digit and difficulty when regrouping is needed (e.g., 10 more than 95). Activities should include hundred chart explorations, base-ten block demonstrations, and mental math games appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.prompt: This topic covers One more or less - up to 120 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(D)). Students learn to identify the number that is one more or one less than a given number, connecting to the counting sequence. Key concepts include understanding that one more means the next counting number and one less means the previous one, and recognizing what happens at decade boundaries (e.g., one more than 39 is 40). Vocabulary: one more, one less, next, before, after, counting sequence. Common misconceptions include difficulty at decade transitions and confusing one more with ten more. Activities should use number lines, hundred charts, and concrete objects appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Comparing: more or fewer?
skills.prompt: This topic covers comparing groups to determine more or fewer for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(E)). Students compare two groups of objects to determine which has more, fewer, or if they have the same amount. Key concepts include matching objects one-to-one between groups, counting each group and comparing the numbers, and using comparison language correctly. Vocabulary: more, fewer, less, same, equal, compare, count, group. Common misconceptions include confusing more and fewer, comparing by visual appearance rather than counting, and difficulty when groups are arranged differently. Activities should include side-by-side group comparisons, matching activities, and real-world comparison situations appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Compare numbers up to 10 using words
skills.prompt: This topic covers Compare numbers up to 10 using words for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(E)). Students express number comparisons using words such as 'greater than,' 'less than,' and 'equal to.' Key concepts include comparing numbers using place value understanding, expressing comparisons in complete sentences, and understanding that comparison is relative (a number can be greater than one number but less than another). Vocabulary: greater than, less than, equal to, compare, more, fewer, same. Common misconceptions include confusing comparison terms and not using place value to compare systematically. Activities should include comparing with base-ten blocks, writing comparison statements, and sorting numbers from least to greatest appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Use place value to compare numbers up to 100
skills.prompt: This topic covers Use place value to compare numbers up to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(E)). Students apply place value understanding to compare multi-digit numbers systematically. Key concepts include starting comparison from the largest place value, moving to smaller place values only when digits are equal, and understanding that a digit in a higher place value position is worth more than any combination of digits in lower positions. Vocabulary: place value, compare, greater than, less than, equal to, digit, ones, tens, hundreds. Common misconceptions include comparing digits without considering place value, comparing from right to left, and thinking longer numbers are always greater. Activities should include place value chart comparisons, using base-ten blocks to compare, and ordering sets of numbers appropriate for ages 6-7.

Vocabulary: commutative property, order, addend, sum, turn-around facts. Common misconceptions include thinking this applies to subtraction and not recognizing opportunities to apply it. skills.prompt: This topic covers Add by counting on - sums up to 10 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.3(D)). Counting on is an addition strategy where students start from the larger addend and count up by the smaller addend. For example, for 8+3, start at 8 and count: 9, 10, 11. Key concepts include starting from the larger number for efficiency, keeping track of how many counts were made, and recognizing when counting on is the best strategy. Vocabulary: count on, start, addend, sum, strategy, efficient. Common misconceptions include starting from 1 instead of the first addend, including the starting number in the count, and not choosing the larger number to start from. Activities should include number line jumps, counting on with fingers, and comparing counting on with other strategies appropriate for ages 6-7.

Key concepts include understanding addition as combining, the commutative property, and strategies like counting on, making ten, doubles, and near-doubles. Vocabulary: add, addend, sum, plus, equals, fact, fluency, doubles, near doubles. Students should progress from concrete models to mental computation. Common misconceptions include always counting from 1, not using the commutative property, and confusing addition with subtraction. Activities should include timed practice, flashcards, fact family triangles, and strategy-based games appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Take apart numbers in different ways - sums up to 10
skills.prompt: This topic covers Take apart numbers in different ways - sums up to 10 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.3(D)). Take-apart problems present a total that must be decomposed into two parts. Unlike take-away problems, nothing is removed; instead, a known quantity is separated into groups. Key concepts include understanding that the total equals the sum of the parts, writing subtraction sentences to show one part when the total and other part are known, and recognizing multiple valid decompositions. Vocabulary: take apart, decompose, parts, whole, total, subtraction sentence. Common misconceptions include confusing take-apart with take-away, not understanding that multiple decompositions exist, and difficulty with the equation structure. Activities should include physically separating groups of objects, writing equations for each decomposition, and exploring all ways to take apart a number appropriate for ages 6-7.

skills.skill: Make a number using addition - sums up to 10
skills.prompt: This topic covers Make a number using addition - sums up to 10 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.3(D)). Students explore different ways to decompose a number into two parts, building flexibility with number composition. Key concepts include finding all combinations that make a target number, recording combinations as equations, understanding that the same number can be decomposed in multiple ways, and recognizing the commutative property. Vocabulary: decompose, compose, parts, whole, ways to make, combination. Common misconceptions include missing some combinations, not recognizing commutative pairs (3+5 and 5+3), and thinking there is only one way to make a number. Activities should include systematic exploration with manipulatives, recording all combinations, and connecting to fact families appropriate for ages 6-7.

Common misconceptions include confusing this with addition, losing track of counts, and not recognizing when this strategy is most efficient (when numbers are close together). Activities should include open number line jumps, comparing strategies, and practice with numbers close together appropriate for ages 6-7.
